reviews.review_text:
I really wanted to like X4: Foundations, because on paper this is exactly the kind of game I should love: start with one ship, build your way up, create trade networks, hire people, own stations, command fleets and eventually become a real force in the universe.

The problem is not the concept. The concept is excellent. The problem is that the game often feels like it actively fights against the player.

The early experience is especially bad. Instead of giving you a clear and satisfying first path into the sandbox, the game throws you into confusing objectives, poor guidance and missions that can be completely inappropriate for a new player. Being sent to locate and scan a Xenon base almost immediately is not "freedom", it is bad onboarding. A new player should not need to already understand half the game’s systems just to avoid wasting time or flying into a death trap.

Mission discovery is also terrible. You can sit at a major station, open mission offers and simply get nothing. No direction, no useful alternative, no clear explanation. The answer seems to be "fly around and maybe something appears." That is not depth. That is friction. A sandbox still needs a functional early-game loop. CGo wander around and hope the game starts" is not good design.

The user interface is another major problem. X4 often feels less like a space game and more like administrative software with spaceships attached. Basic things are needlessly awkward. Saving the game, renaming things, finding options, understanding what is happening on the map, figuring out what the game actually wants from you, too much of it feels clumsy, inconsistent or badly explained.

And this is the core issue: I want to play a game, not clock into a second job.

Complexity is not the problem. Factorio is complex. Good strategy games are complex. Deep simulations can be complex. But good complexity gives you tools, clarity and momentum. X4 too often gives you confusion, hidden rules and bad presentation, then expects the player to excuse it because the simulation underneath is ambitious.

There may be a great game buried here. I can believe that. I can believe that after tutorials, guides, YouTube videos, forum searches, mods and many hours of trial and error, X4 becomes impressive. But that is not a defense of the game. That is an admission that the game does a poor job of introducing and respecting the player.

Before buying DLC, players should seriously ask themselves whether they actually enjoy the base game’s systems, interface and pacing, or whether they are buying the fantasy of what X4 could be. More content does not fix a weak early experience, bad guidance or a UI that constantly gets in the way.

If you are already deeply invested in X4 and enjoy fighting through its systems, then maybe the DLC makes sense. But if you are a new player hoping for a smooth "start small and build an empire" space sandbox, be careful. The promise is fantastic. The execution can be exhausting.

For me, X4 is not bad because it lacks ambition. It is bad because it mistakes poor accessibility, weak onboarding and clumsy interface design for depth. A game can be deep without being hostile to basic enjoyment. X4 too often forgets that.

you will learn early that theres no upkeep for ships, the game seens hardcore but its actually casual theres no rush, its all the clunkness of the UI and poor design of interactions that keep people away.

pros: its a good singleplayer game, space simulator, empire sim, manager sim, RTS-ish, lots of mods, that you can sink thousands of hours in

cons: you will need a youtube tab open to learn the game, not intuitive even for experienced gamers, there are no planets to visit, theres no upkeep for ships, no salary for workers, no fuel to be paid, ITS TOO EASY AND TOO GRINDY AT THE SAME TIME (its takes a lot of money and a lot of resources being made/trasported for galactic empires to run)

funny: your ship has a tractor beam to loot things in space, but for some reason, some items required you to get out of the ship (in space) and PUT THE LOOT IN YOUR POCKETS PERSONALLY, also the SCANNER in the ship requires you to BUMP INTO THINGS to capture RADIO signals, thats what i mean with clunky mechanics AND THERE ARE MORE

This game is amazing.  I just do wanna say that I started it on a Wednesday, played for an hour did some tutorials, made a terran cadet.  See i bought this in 2020 - and bought the DLCs on sale, all on sale over time.  Now 8 years into development. 

Ive been waiting for this game since 2003 Freelancer.

Anyway started on a wednesday and just couldnt get too into it.  Thursday tried again did more tutorials and just...I just wasn't hooked.  Oh well...guess this isn't it.

Friday morning.  What do you want to game?  I'm on a long vacation.

X4 my brain said, I want to learn a bit more.

I haven't stopped playing since that Friday morning

Did you enjoy Star Citizen? Stellaris? Hearts of iron?
or any strategy games with a vertical learning curve?

This game is literally everything i ever wanted and i was scared for years of spending time getting into it.
It's much simpler than what i expected, just do the basic tutorials (30 min) and the map one, thats IT.

or you can press H whenever and do the tutorial for whatever you're curious about mid game, and drop straight back into the game when you complete it.

Just play the damn game.

This game could be the best space game ever made (BUT) right now, I’m done forcing myself to play it.

X4 has some of the best-looking ships in any space game. The scale is incredible, the capital ships look amazing, and the combat can be genuinely awesome when everything finally comes together. The problem is that the game gives you all these beautiful ships, fleets, carriers, destroyers, and battleships… but almost no meaningful reason to actually use them.

That is what kills the experience for me.

War should matter. Building a fleet should matter. Owning powerful ships should open the door to exciting, dangerous, rewarding gameplay. Instead, most of the time it feels like I am creating my own fun while the game refuses to give me a real reason to fight. The universe has all the tools for epic space warfare, but not enough motivation, reward, or consequence behind it.

Even bounty hunting feels bad. If one of my fleet ships gets the kill, it does not even count properly toward my police license reward. Why build a fleet, then punish the player when the fleet does its job?

Guild missions are also not worth it most of the time. If I lose even one fighter during a combat mission, the reward often does not cover the loss. That makes combat feel pointless instead of exciting. I should be encouraged to take risks, use my ships, and go to war — not punished for engaging with the most cinematic part of the game.

This is the frustrating part: the game is so close to greatness. The ships are there. The combat is there. The sandbox is there. But the reasons to use those things are weak.

Until this changes, I’m keeping my negative review. If the developers finally make war, bounty hunting, guild missions, fleet combat, and late-game military gameplay actually rewarding and meaningful, I will gladly change this to a positive review and call it one of the best space game ever made.

But right now, it feels like the game gives me a legendary fleet and then forgets to give me a war worth fighting.

I'd sue the devs for this addictiong piece of virtual drug if I had the slightest chance of winning. Don't buy it unless you're interested in losing interest in a lot of stuff you used to enjoy.

Oh and make sure to get VRO and Reemergence mods, you can thank me later.

This game looks great fun and is beautiful. I really wanted to enjoy playing this game. I have tried for over 20 hours to get one moment of enjoyment out of it. I have not done that for even a second. There was always one more small problem to be fixed before I could get into it and enjoy playing. These problems were nothing to do with getting it to work on my PC, they were all with the gameplay.

Every little thing you need to know to be able to play the game requires you to stop playing the game and look up what to do elsewhere (the game tells you nothing). You then go back to the game, play for three seconds and discover another problem that requires you to stop playing and look up what to do elsewhere. The tutorials are plain useless and require you to stop playing the game to go back to them. If you have forgotten one slight part of the extremely dense tutorials, you have to stop playing the game and go and do the tutorial again. This means that 20 hours of play I have is actually easily double that in looking things up to try and work out how to do anything.

The developers apparently decided that 'gameplay' should include playing unpaid tech support for brain-dead AI pilots. I commissioned an automated mining ship, and the game let it sit idle in space for hours without a single error message or notification. It didn't tell me it lacked a mining laser. It didn't tell me it couldn't find resources. It didn't tell me it couldn't find somewhere to sell the resources when I finally got it to mine some. It just sat there silently wasting my time while I had to guess the hidden mechanics.

X4 doesn't have a learning curve; it has an interrogation policy. It hides its basic rules, ignores your controller inputs until you find a hidden master switch, and forces you to constantly look things up on a wiki just to make a basic menu function work.

I am not exaggerating when I say that at no point in 20 hours of playing did I actually play for more than 1-2 minutes before I had to look something up because something wasn't working and I didn't have any clues in game as to why not or what to do about it.

Cant recommend this game enough.

The learning curve is steep, but it scratches a space sim itch that no other game does.

The X Series... One of the best games ever made.
If you buy an X game you get many many years of updates and support for it without cashgrab dlcs.
Egosoft is one of the few companies the whole industry should take as a positive example.
I will buy everything they put out just to support their business model.
The game is a giant space sandbox with endless potential hours of gameplay.

A good game otherwise ruined by awful voice acting and self-destructive design, and a complete disrespect for the player's time. 

Every single story mission feels like a mission from Temu, with zero thought going in to the writing and characters.
The meat of the game resides in the economics simulation, but in order to fully interact with that part of the game you have to funnel your way through these awful mission "subways" to access more content; and the majority of the time its just an awful experience. 

Couple that with bad performance, bad visuals, debilitating save-load times, and a flight model that feels like you're flying through mud, and you get an overall unenjoyable experience.
yeah you get sucked in, this is NOT a casual game - be prepared to have to plan things... That said its, well vast in scope with plenty of action for action nerds and tons of strategy for RTS nerds

A massive sandbox with some story beats in it.
The game is as fun as you are willing to engage with it as a simulation.

From Pilot to Puppet Master: The Ultimate Simulation.

Most space games stop at "buy low, sell high." X4: Foundations goes much further. The recent diplomacy updates have changed the game for me—you aren't just a participant in the galaxy; you are its architect.

With the new diplomacy system, you can research and deploy agents to trigger "Diplomatic Incidents." There is nothing more satisfying than watching two long-standing, friendly allies turn on each other because of a few well-placed sparks you ignited. If a faction’s growth is threatening your trade routes, you don't always have to declare war yourself; you can just trick their neighbors into doing the dirty work for you.

The best X game Egosoft have made so far. The interface takes a little getting used to, but once done the universe is your playground. I have been a pirate, a trader, explorer, captain of industry and there is still more to do.

The universe is alive and changing. One playtrough, the Xenon were pushed back without me lifting a finger. My current game, the HOP made a spirited defence, but are almost completely overrun with Xenon. Without my fleet guarding it, Antigone would be next.

Throguhly recommend. Egosoft did an amazing job.

The best space game/empire management game I ever played. IT's a got a fully realistic economy that works too that isn't simulated.

Every game in this series has been bomb shells and soooo good. But x4 foundaitons was the first one with a fully realstic economy that wasn't simulated.
Absolutely would. Be what you want and let your legacy build it's self! I normally play on a i7 14700F and RTX 4060 8GB. but this has straight GPU i'm testing to swap for a beautiful 4GB or 6GB GPU

I have played all mainline X games since X2, and surprise, X4 is another X game.

TLDR.

If you like the X series than you will like X4. It streamlines much from the earlier games to the point that installing mods is no longer required to make the game playable. In particular, maps, menus, and logistics/fleet automation is greatly improved.

Not so TLDR.

On the positive side. This is an X game, with all that the long time fans have learned to expect.

Its a space-sim inside a trading sim inside a logistics sim inside a strategy game. Its an open-world sandbox economy, and you have a space ship. Come for the space combat, stay for the excel spreadsheets used to optimize factory inputs.

There is a plot, but it is not the main focus. Rather, story missions are tutorials and signposts to guide you on your way through the many (many) things you can do in this game. Egosoft have had years to slowly bolt on an increasing number of features to the X series and at this point there sure is a lot to do.

You have to decide what you want to get out of this game. Do you want to try the life of a pirate? You can do that. Do you want to fight off the onslaught of the hostile terraforming fleet? You can do that too. Or do you want to build a trading empire with chains of interconnected factory stations feeding your always growing production lines and the logistical nightmare of keeping it all running? Nothing is stopping you, but you are going to need a war fleet to keep it all safe. Good thing you can build that too.

On the negative side. This is an X game, with all that the long time fans have learned to expect.

Story? It exists.
Bugs? There are plenty.
Voice acting? You have heard better.
Quality? Surprisingly good for an X game, but don't expect a AAA here.

And finally: Ease of entry.

There are more shortcuts in X4 to get you started, and some tutorials that are actually decent, but this is a glacially paced game that doesn't explain anything to the player. You better have a hundred hours to spare and you better find some guides.

All in all, if you like X games then this is possibly the best one. It is far from perfect. It has issues that would be deal-breakers for another game. But there are no other games like the X series. If you like this particular sub-genre of space-sims then this is it. Its the best you can get.
I'm loving this game at the moment, being a pirate hunter and building a mega factory station. its much more stable than I remember it being.

Honestly give this game like 20-30 hours, I also didnt like it at first but you gotta get into a bit before it gets fun and if you after 30 hours still dont like the game thats fine but please give it the chance it deserves because this game is genuiely amazing,

I cannot stress how truly terrible the interactions in this game are. I've made a real effort to get into this game, I've really tried to get through the introduction but it is just.. bad.

To start, I did the tutorials. Sure, get the controls down, basic flight mechanics. Then you run a few missions that involve a lot of just flying in a straight line and jumping through gates to get around. People talk at you, and you cannot skip these conversations if you've had them before. One of the first few missions has you opening lock boxes. How, you ask? Great question. You have to shoot them in a very specific way. I had to look it up because it wouldn't work for me, my range wasn't just right.

After a few more dawdling missions and unskippable dialogue, you follow a signal through a gate and end up near a hostile station. Don't get too close or it will kill you, and if you didn't manually save you'll be sent back quite a ways.

If you aren't killed by the station, there's some dialogue and some...things happen? It's not made clear what happens, and I couldn't see what happened. Cool. Anyway I go back through the gate and do some combat, and then I have to sit and wait for a timer to run out before I can continue on. I ended up sitting around for almost three minutes just watching it tick down. Thrilling.

Next I am sent on a mission to meet up with a ship. I have to "find my way" there, which involves either exploring the sector to find the gate to the next part, or opening comms to other ships and asking them, which I had to figure out by searching online. You do this three or four times before you arrive at the actual ship.

The NPC on the ship gives me a fetch quest at a nearby station, and off I go. But wait, it's not on the trade menu at all. No, it's on the black market, which is in the bar, which I wouldn't have known if I hadn't looked it up online. Again. And if you don't have the credits to buy it, well, you're out of luck, figure it out, it's not cheap at this point in the game. 

Get back, turn in the fetched thing, and off I go to repair some satellites. I fly to them, I get out, I repair them, I get back in, I repeat this a few times. I dock, run to the NPC, get back on my ship, fly back out, and repair more things.  Great.

Fast forward slightly, I'm trying to add modules to a station. It says I need Builders. I click "Add Builders" and it opens up the map. It provides no further instruction. What is a builder? Where do I find them? These are structured missions introducing game mechanics, and it provides almost no information at all. I have to look online to find out that a Builder is a ship type I have to actually FIND and hire somehow. 

I've tried so hard to get into this game and have fun with it, but this game is seemingly not interested in being fun. The introductory missions are slow, boring, and do a terrible job telling you anything about what you're supposed to be doing. 

I've poured thousands of hours into Elite Dangerous and Star Sector and I love, LOVE space sims. This game is a huge disappointment. The combat is clunky, the interface is clumsy, the controls don't allow for multiple buttons so controller support isn't great, the introduction to mechanics is what I would generously call "half-assed", and I've been met with frustration at every turn. I feel like I'm playing an early build, and as much as I want to try and get through this, the game doesn't seem to care about that half as much as I do.
I bought this cuz x3 was one of my favorite games, but the time invest to actual fun ratio sucks. Don't buy if you come off of x3, this is nothing similar and is closer to a simulator than a game.
Great game, with a learning curve so steep that you could classify it as a wall.
The game is so menu heavy and confusing I couldn't get through the tutorials.

I love this game and its atmosphere. I love chill mining and producing wares to support one faction and make it dominant. Keep in mind you will need very good processor for big fleets and many ships! :)
Recent updates are awesome and long time waited !

Best Regards for Egosoft ! thumbs up

The X series is probably my favorite gaming franchise. It has a semi-steep learning curve, but it allows me to tap into so many things that define the type of gamer I am. The developer is awesome; I truly hope they never sell out or lose their identity. For those of us who play, the X series is a significant part of our lives.

This game offers so many "roleplay" opportunities: from waging war against evil AI robots, to fighting the humans who created them, to clashing with non-evil humans who had nothing to do with the robots but are at war with them anyway. You can even choose not to go to war at all, simply watching the conflict unfold from the background while you mine an asteroid.

There is so much to do, whether you're building a space empire from your personal office or fighting for scraps in a forgotten system. While there is plenty of RNG, there are countless ways to play X4. I highly recommend it; if you feel intimidated, there are many videos and guides for every aspect of the game provided by a very loving community.

It is also important to note that version 9.0 is coming out soon. It feels like a love letter from the developers, polishing the game so it remains playable for as long as possible. I assume we will see X5 on the horizon eventually, but if you are thinking of trying X4 now, it is currently at its peak. It is a fantastic experience compared to years ago; a massive amount of work has gone into the game, and that dedication continues today.
One of the best space sim on the market with unique, german-based technical and gameplay solutions. Totaly love it.

I absolutely love this game, I've done everything from humble mining runs to capturing massive capital ships. I've completed most of the storylines and am still working through the rest. There is just so much content here.
Station Building: surprisingly relaxing and deeply satisfying to design
Fleet Combat: Nothing beats the feeling of sending an entire fleet to tear down an enemy station 
The Progression: Watching your empire grow from a single ship to a massive galactic power is fantastic
You simply never get bored, if you love deep space sims, you need this game!!

As you read this you may feel that my opinions as offered are critical of the game, however, I actually do think that this can be a fun game and is worth trying for yourself - even if just the base game. What I hope to do here is to expand on some of the nuance that is either overlooked, ignored or just not acknowledged in many other reviews (be they on Steam or in YouTube videos).

To begin the game is both visually impressive as well as being fairly well optimized for its universe size and the number of things being simulated. There are specific situations that can expose some performance issues but often those are both later in the game play through and also (usually) are easy to work around. Egosoft is also very active in both paid DLC that in my opinion have content that justifies the price as well as free updates that improve the basic game systems. Recent examples are the Flight Model update that improved the dogfighting and the 9.0 update that is making significant improvements to both the behavior of large and extra-large ships as well as reworking the balance and flexibility of small and medium ship load-outs. The DLC it is not in any way required, the core game is complete. The DLCs add new sectors (areas of space) and factions, as well as ships (either as part of a faction or as one offs) but do not drastically change the basic gameplay, rather they just add "more".

The first thing you have to come to terms with is the fact that there are two (connected but almost independent) games within the sandbox.

The first of these that you will encounter is the dog-fight simulator. The basic premise of the game is that you start from a basic one seat fighter and via a host of different activities you build your personal organization out to be a galactic power.

The second "game" opens up when you have a larger and more prosperous organization behind you. At this point your gameplay shifts to more of an organizational leader rather than a pilot. You still have the option to jump in the cockpit and take the fight to the enemy but more of your time will be spent issuing orders and making high level decisions about resource gathering, combat deployments and logistics.

The plots (quest lines) in the game either explicitly or implicitly require a small or medium ship to complete (a vast majority of the time it is restricted to a small ship). I can appreciate the limitations that Egosoft has, being an independent self published studio, as well as the design goals that make this reasonable, even effective. The issue is that at times it feels clunky, and pulls me out of the mental mode I am in and forces me into a different conceptual framework. The intent seems to be that YOU are the hero and only YOU can do certain things or you have to be personally involved in some way or other. And in the early game that fits the "mode" you are in (as you don't have any backup) once you have a significant organization behind you feels less rewarding and more chore-like.

In contrast to the "hero pilot" missions other steps or plots require either huge fiscal investment (buying stuff from NPCs) or massive logistical support and supporting infrastructure. For example there are several "build us a new station" or "provide a fleet" objectives. Both of which will require a large organization of one kind or another.

Neither of these systems are poorly developed. However, they don't feel unified to me. Sometimes you are the Hero Pilot sometimes you are the CEO. While they do interact it feels like most of the time they require completely independent systems and do not feel like they have much to do with each other without the narrative link the game provides. IE You being an ace combat pilot doesn't actually matter to your organization and your massive galactic organization doesn't provide many tangible benefits to your dog-fighting.

All that said, the plots are pretty engaging and in almost all cases have significant impacts on the sandbox, from changing alliances to escalating wars to peacefully ending them. And it does feel good to have personal galactic impact, but because of the way the economy works (more on that in a moment) some choices can adversely effect the feel of the simulation.

So, lets talk about the simulation. First, as is stated the entire universe is legitimately simulated from raw ore collection through refinement, production and final assembly of every ship and station. There are two levels of simulation that can drastically impact a number of things. The bulk of the universe is going to be in a simplified mathematical simulation mode where in things like collision, turret tracking, and similar high-cost operations are reduced to simple mathematical formulas. The second mode is a strict fully simulated bubble around your personal character.

This does mean that some activities you will *want* to be in the system for and others are actually *better* if you are somewhere else when they are happening. Mining and fleet level combat are two places where this becomes very obvious. Which is to say that the same combat encounter can have *very* different outcomes if your are or are not physically present or that the real-world rate at which you miners fill up will change based on your presence.

This combination of CEO/Hero Pilot bifurcation and the simulation differences take some getting used to and you often have to manage your personal activity based on those differences rather than just what you want to be doing.

Now, about that economic system....

So, the game is very complex - not complicated. The economy is not hard to understand - it revolves entirely around destruction. If there isn't anything dying (be it stations or ships) the entire economy stops functioning. They have introduced things like Terraforming (basically an economic/logistical "end game") as well as a military crisis that provides a combat "end game". This means that for the most "alive" feeling galaxy you realistically want every faction to be at war with every other faction. And you either are also in the middle of the pit slapping people or are playing the arms dealer keeping the factions fed for their endless wars.

They have introduced things like a UN-style diplomatic system where you can interact with the various factions to manipulate their relationships with other factions as well as your own. Including both direct diplomacy as well as espionage. And while I was initially excited about that patch, the basic reality of the economy means that these systems are only there to help you manage who is killing who rather than working toward some greater outcome.

There are some awkward parts, like the UI though this is being actively improved, and some opaque systems that you only really can learn about outside the actual game as well as some "split personality" baked into the design. Some of this friction Egosoft in various ways have acknowledged and are working on improving, some of it is just part of the game that has to be accepted as is.

If you can get past some of the rough parts or can learn to manage them (usually doable in some way or other, but not always) there is a lot of flexibility in how you interact with the universe that can provide a ton of fun. That's not even to mention the fact that many of these elements that I discussed can be modded since the game supports Steam mods and the community is really quite active from my experience. You can also get mods from 3rd party sources but I rarely do so with any steam game unless required.

In summary, X4 is fun, or at least can be, as long as you are willing to accept some limitations and awkwardness and are willing to spend time on it both in and out of game. It legitimately does allow a broad range of play-styles and player directed objectives in the pure sandbox portions. But you really have to approach it less as a "game" and more like a "toy".
Its an expansive political, economic, explorative sandbox that you can take in any direction. Highly recommend.

Pretty great game once you get past the initial learning curve, though there's still a lot of parts of the game where outside information feels necessary. Tried this game once a very long time ago and it didn't really click until I tried it again a couple of years ago with the Kingdom End DLC which is when I put in most of my hours. Picking it up again and slowly getting back into it.

While it has native Linux support I find that Proton generally performs much better, but there are times when launching the game that the splash screen hangs and nothing happens but killing it through Steam and relaunching seems to fix it.

The game is simply unparalleled as a space tycoon / fleet commander fantasy, particularly in the sense of scope it offers.

Flying your fighter next to a massive capital ship's turret bigger than your entire ship. Watching space battles from the flight deck of a carrier. Observing your fleet of trading ships coming and going from your station.

The game isn't perfect. The story missions are mostly just serviceable. Several game systems aren't particularly engaging, particularly the crew system is underdeveloped and tediously manual. 

It's generally not a hard game and as you reach the "end game", the limits of the somewhat static "playground" universe start to show.

The journey is very much worth it though. Building up your empire is fun, there's a wide variety of ships and activities to choose from and the graphics, while no longer entirely fresh, are good enough to enjoy the space battles.
fun space game can do fights or trade. LOT to learn and master
One of my favorite games, Freelancer on steroids without the arbitrary restrictions.

addicting as hell, once you get past the control scheme, follow every tutorial before playing!

the DLC make it 10x more complex and fun so i recommend starting a new playthrough with the dlc once you've come to love this game.

it'll likely take a beefcake to run this game (with good graphics) so beware!

10/10 x4 is my newest obsession

I played this back when it first came to early access. It was a good game with potential then. 
I recently came back to it, and i gotta say, it is living up to that potential.
Flight controls are a bit different from most space sims. 
But the living world and role playing aspects of the game are top tier.
So much content, you could spend hundreds of hours in game and still not scratch the surface.
Don't forget about the mods as well. There is a lot of cheaty mods but if you scroll past those, you'll find a lot of helpful and immersive mods.
I would recommend that you have at least 32 gigs of ram if you plan to play a long campaign. The simulation eats ram later in the game when you have thousands of ships and stations all being simulated at once.
Realy steep learning curve but once you get past that it's fun. You can do a lot in the game even without buying any DLCs.

This game is awesome ! IF they push it to the next level where it integrates details of ship maintenance same as Star Citizen and some planet exploration, some encounters FPS of boarding capital ships ...this would break all other games

This is a game where you'll feel lost the first time you go in. I've played a few different times by way of running through the tutorials (which are really good on teaching "how" to do certain things) however, after the tutorials you ask yourself what's next. This last time I decided to give X4 another shot I told myself skip the tutorials and jump in. I looked up how to do things on my way and found this a more enjoyable approach.

I found watching "Dia Builds" on YouTube. He has some very good beginner tips, specifically the No DLC play through. My biggest thing was I'd like a space station and start working at that.

As time has gone on, I am finding this game has so much depth. It's space exploration, there's a full economy system, management system, space station building and so on. You can build multiple space stations by the way. I don't know this game has hit so much different than the last play throughs. It's a good game.

This is it. I have been gaming since my dad bought our first computer in 2005 or something. I fell in love with gaming with "Spider-Man 2: The Game" at that time since I really really loved Spider Man and now, I could play as him, web around and just immerse myself as him. Then I started playing all kinds of games, games that I found on my cousin's computer, friends' computers... I played the first Call of Duty games and loved the atmosphere, the way that it took me to times that I had no idea of. Making me feel like the soldiers there. Then my grandfather bought me Sims 2, It was a bit confusing since I was little but still, I loved the world that I was creating, making people hangout, building stuff... Then my parents got me a Playstation 2 at my birthday, which was a BIG thing at that time. It came with Tekken 5 and man, I played the hell out of it. It was a fighting game but I kinda cared more about characters and their stories and was just playing to unlock more characters so I could see more stories. Other than computer games, I also had plastic toy soldiers that I was creating stories with, giving some of them extreme plot armor and just, creating my own stories, my own world. Then came Metin 2 that I saw from my uncle, which was played by almost everyone at that time. An mmorpg that I can roleplay, it was a big gift for me. Fast forward to future, I fell in love with gaming more and more with games like Fallout New Vegas, Skyrim, The Witcher 3, Hitman games, Mount and Blade games, Kenshi and so much more that it would take hours for me to count them all. Sure I loved mindlessly playing games, shooting stuff sometimes but the real thing about gaming has always been creating my own characters, my own worlds or immerse myself as someone for me. I adored games that gave me an option to do whatever I want such as Mount and Blade, Kenshi, Survivalist: Invisible Strain, Morrowind... Then came X4: Foundations. At first I played the tutorials for awhile and got overwhelmed a bit, so I let go of it for like a month. Then I came back, just throwing myself inside the game this time, learning things as I go, just like how it was back then when I was playing Hitman: Blood Money and was trying to figure what to do and how to do something or playing GODS: Lands of Infinity and trying to figure out how I could dodge certain enemies or have enough power to kill them. I realised that learning things as I go made me get used to them faster and the steep learning hill became a cozy walk. But the real thing that made me fall in love with X4 and gaming overall one more time is that this game was giving me everything I wanted from a game. A world thats running by itself where you are a person capable of doing beautiful things, or terrible things according to your playstyle. A world with so much opportunities, so much lore to immerse yourself with. Faction relations that actually matters and can shape your character accordingly. I immediately bought all the DLC's, all bonus contents, whatever there is. I just love this universe and what it offers you. A true sandbox where you can be anything or anyone you want. I even went as far as getting the books of the universe because wow, this is what gaming is supposed to be. Thank you for everything you have done for this game and all that there will be. Thank you for making me fall in love with gaming again and your universe. This is easily the best game I have ever played and although every game has its special place in my heart for me, this game deserves the biggest place there is. Thank you Egosoft...

Great game. 

If you like space games with complexity and freedom, this game is for you.
Difficult to understand the first few hours but gradually im getting used to it, love it

This game is super, super deep- but the learning curve is super, super steep.  It kind of is Wing Commander: Privateer, combined with a city builder/economic engine building/trading game (on a galactic scale), Mount and Blade, scaling into a space RTS game, scaling into a Stellaris like grand strategy game.  You can play it as a space flight sim fan, a defense economics nerd  (want to corner the market on a particular good that an enemy faction needs to produce ships?  You can!  Want to sell ships, or goods to a second faction to support them in a war against a faction you don't like?  You can!).  

You can be a pirate.  You can be a mercenary.  You can be an ore magnate.  You can sign up for missions in a faction on faction war (once your rep is high enough with the faction you want to join).  

I would agree though that the early game is kind of rough until you figure out how to make money (strongly recommend you watch some youtube videos about automining if you're new).  Many of the more substantive faction missions are nigh on impossible until you have a proper fleet or starting capital to undergo the requested mission; but a lot of this game (in fact, one of the most satisfying things about this game) is basically making and executing your own plans- For example:

Genocidal enemy faction holds a system between two different factions.  Left unchecked, genocidal faction will likely kill both factions over time (and thereby diminish your trading opportunities and/or threaten stations you yourself have built).  Genocidal faction periodically sends out capital ship task forces with some fighter support to basically pound whatever stations or ships it finds, and attempts to build defensive stations on the jump points into the both faction system to establish footholds to advance forward.  

If you assist either faction with your current military forces, you can beat back the offensive task forces that jump through, but it is costly, and you don't have the forces to take the system that the enemy is jumping out of.  The most economical way to defend a system is to build a defense station outside of the jump point the enemy is coming from; which would not only take pressure off of your fleet and the friendly faction fleets, but it might also allow you to build up your fleet and go on the offensive. 

The problem is that you need to get resources to the sites you are building the stations at to get the stations built.  That not only requires you to defend station sites until the stations are built, but if one faction has limited access to resources to purchase to build the stations, you are going to have to arrange resource convoys of your own transport ships to the station build sites.  

You then have to ask yourself, "well, if I fight through enemy space, it's two jumps, and I can accomplish it in a couple minutes, but if I don't go through enemy space, it's eight jumps, and it's going to be the better part of an hour just to get the supply ships to the build site, let alone build the station- can the factions or myself hold out that long, or does it make sense to send a scout through enemy space, see if I can blitz some fast transports through there (heck, I can personally try and fly it through myself if I want to), and have a couple warships waiting to jump in and bail out the transports if they get shot out of hyperspace?

That is one example of some of the interesting decision making and "self made" missions you get to make.  

All and all, its a pretty great game; IF you are good at self direction, IF you like the idea of using markets as a weapon, or IF you fantasized about calling in a couple of capship and fighter squadrons to wreck face in the missions in TIE Fighter where it was basically you by yourself up against half of Rebellion or Admiral Zaarin's traitorous dogs.  :D

There are lore and story missions in the game; as well as special ships and locations to find, but I would say the main draw is the sandbox, which is dynamic with every new play (the board doesn't change, but the factions may behave differently and be more or less militarily or economically aggressive); meaning no two games are exactly the same. 

There really is nothing else like it.

Great game, lots of fun. Recommend Mods to make up for some of the vanilla versions weaker aspects.

This game is incredibly complicated, well in excess of stereotypically complicated games like eve or even really infamous ones like Aurora4x, however with that complexity is such a good, well made game where complexity is a result of the depth and quality of the experience and not there for the simple sake of complexity. I also love that the dlc is generally well worth the money and the major updates that release upon some dlc releases actually contain the majority of the content, NOT the way paradox does it where the dlc contains almost all of the content of the update.

As far as performance goes, the graphics are kinda middle of the road. The gpu performance isnt bad at all but also isnt notably great. As you will see in my system specs I have a really good cpu (especially for this kind of sim-heavy game) and that invalidates any statements I can make about the cpu performance of this game. Just know that this game is quite cpu heavy due to the incredibly indepth nature of the simulation.

Game is also pretty moddable which is nice. Tons of content or ships or whatever you can add to the already very diverse setting of this game.

I loved the previous X games up to X3.  I skipped the last one and had a hard time getting into this one.  After getting through the tutorial I would quite.  It seems to have less guidance etc.

If you put the time in the game is absolutely fantastic.   The way the universe evolves etc is well worth the inventment.   Online Tutorials will be your friend but there are some really well done videos out there.

It has really pulled me in.  Going into a sector for quick scrap and pulling out is great fun.  They might chase you for a bit and it seemed like cheesing the game to retreat to a friendly base. . . until you realize it is realistic and that you are part of a larger conflict.   

It seemed like it was going to be a failry stale progression loop until some Xenon took intrest in my first factory. . likely shouldn't have build it there . . good money though and defending it is fun.  

Runs great.

This game is legit. Maybe the most interesting and satisfying game I have ever played. Taking a small break after putting in a few hundred hours obsessively before I return post 9.0

I would agree with the Kenshi in space comparisons. I'm generally not a big space world game player, but I love this one. The systems and opportunities to play are all so satisfying. Some of the missions I could do without, but the sandbox world and what you can do is what I love about it so much. Hard to explain, kind of like Kenshi, but it's just such a fun game.

Overall I’m having fun but as a new player ot can be frustrating trying to figure out the most basic systems. The tutorials help but the learning curve is still a bit steep. Not as bad as Eve Online but maybe similar to elite dangerous, maybe a little more complex
Besides the ship AI in combat its a really good Space simulation for Trade, Fight, Build, Think.
The game gives a lot of nonsensical and impossible missions to start out such as fixing a data leak but not providing any means to fix it. It requires a lot of googling.
Star Citizen wishes it was anywhere close to being this feature complete and good to play.

X4 ist ein Spiel, das einen am Anfang aktiv hasst.

Das UI ist sperrig, die Tutorials reichen nur bedingt, die KI macht manchmal Dinge, die man keinem Menschen erklären kann, und die ersten Stunden bestehen oft aus Verwirrung, Menüs und „Warum funktioniert das jetzt nicht?“.

Aber: Wenn man sich durchbeißt, bekommt man eine der besten Weltraum-Sandboxen überhaupt.

Handel, Mining, Stationsbau, Flottenmanagement, Piraterie, Krieg, eigene Wirtschaftsketten – X4 gibt einem extrem viel Freiheit. Man startet klein und kann irgendwann ein eigenes Imperium aufbauen, inklusive Produktionsnetzwerk und Kampfverbänden.

Es ist nicht perfekt. Es ist nicht einsteigerfreundlich. Es ist nicht immer elegant.

Aber es ist verdammt tief, verdammt umfangreich und auf seine eigene kaputte Art ziemlich genial.

[b]Empfohlen für Leute mit Geduld. Nicht empfohlen für Leute, die nach 20 Minuten alles verstanden haben wollen.[/b]
Keeping it short, this is a simplified, single-player EVE and I love it

Yes this game has a steep learning curve, but if you stick with it you will find it very rewarding. I love the customisation features for both ships and station complexes. I think there is still more egosoft can give to this series in future releases which is an exciting prospect.

Just got the base game at first, then got a bunch of the DLCs a few days latter. The base game is good, the expansions adds so much more and if you can get them for 60% off they are worth it. The new faction ships look so much better.

I've played this game on and off for 8 years and I keep coming back so clearly I like it, but man I cant help but feel like this game is the embodiment of wasted potential. It's SO close to being one of those perfect games for autists to gush about but it seems to keep missing the mark.

There is no real threat in the game, the Xenon have been neutered and require mods to be a threat, pirates are more of a tax and when the player has a self sufficient economy you can simply steamroll every ai faction not that there is literally any reason to do so as territory control is pretty much just painting a sector green.
 
The story missions or plots are truly awful and the devs seem to keep leaning more into them with each dlc to the point of completely blocking off the boron with a questline and whatever they were smoking with the timelines expansion.

As it stands the game is unique, for what it sets out to do it has effectively no competition hence why I am sticking around but if someone ever set out to make a competitor I and I imagine many others would immediately drop it and never look back. Egosoft do regularly make improvements to the game but I have begun to loose faith that my core issues with the game will be addressed and that the game will remain aimless.

I don't know how they do it. I Admire Egosoft. I had this game when it first game out. I can say- This team is dedicated to this project. I am extremely confident that this game will only get better. If they were running low on money- I would 100% donate just to see where this game will go.

Generative AI usage for voice-actors, whether licensed or not, is not something I'm willing to abide. Doesn't matter how good the game is if you're taking a principled stance.

X4 starts with:
‘Cool, I have a little spaceship.’

40 hours later you’re managing supply chains, defending trade routes, monitoring station economies, and yelling at miners like an exhausted middle manager in deep space.

TL;DR = An incomprehensible slop.

Massive 'scale'? More like wasted space. It's large and empty. Even large trader stations and large whatever-you-want-to-call-it is basically void. Dull. Filled with the dullest most unremarkable NPC's you can possibly imagine. 

You will have to waste a bunch of time in micro tutorials. Yes, that's right. You launch 1 micro tutorial for moving your character. One for lifting up and basic flight. One for... you get the point. There is a tutorial for everything. A micro one. 

It's EXHAUSTING and BORING. Two words that perfectly describe the rest of the 'game'.

You want to play this game? sink in hundreds if not thousands of hours? Fine, go ahead, just get yourself checked first. You have deep autism for sure. This game is NOT for a normal human being. It's chore-galore for sure. 

In the most void and boring setting you can imagine.

Invisible walls.

Guard-rails everywhere.

I could go on, but I'm exhausted after my last play session of about 30 minutes which actually felt like 30 years in this bore-fest of a game.

Sorry, not for me... and if it is for you... go get yourself checked. There is something deeply wrong with you. This is like the tedious part of mobas and survival games condensed in the most sterile and boring environments you can possibly imagine. 

Disgusting.

The game itself is well-made and really fun to play. However, I strongly dislike that the Terran faction, which stands for all humanity, adopts a great many Japanese place names such as Tokyo and Osaka. During World War II, Japanese militarism launched wars of aggression, and its troops committed heinous crimes including killing, arson and looting across many regions. What makes it even more unacceptable is that up to now, Japanese authorities still frequently visit the Yasukuni Shrine where Class-A war criminals of WWII are enshrined. They refuse to face history and repent for their crimes, and even glorify past acts of aggression. Given this reality, I cannot approve of such naming choices in the game.
_________________________________________________________________________
Das Spiel selbst ist gut gestaltet und macht wirklich viel Spaß. Dennoch lehne ich es entschieden ab, dass die Terran-Fraktion, welche die gesamte Menschheit repräsentiert, zahlreiche japanische Ortsnamen wie Tokio und Osaka verwendet. Während des Zweiten Weltkriegs entfachte der japanische Militarismus Angriffskriege. Seine Truppen begingen an zahlreichen Orten Verbrechen gegen die Menschlichkeit, darunter Mord, Brandstiftung und Plünderung. Noch unakzeptabler ist die Tatsache, dass die japanischen Behörden bis heute regelmäßig den Yasukuni-Schrein besuchen, in dem Kriegsverbrecher des Zweiten Weltkriegs geehrt werden. Sie weigern sich nach wie vor, die historische Wahrheit anzuerkennen und Reue über ihre Verbrechen zu empfinden, und verherrlichen sogar die einstigen Angriffshandlungen. Vor diesem realen Hintergrund kann ich diese Namensgebung im Spiel keineswegs gutheißen.

At this point so much has been said about this game I almost want to say nothing. But, I will say this: After 4800 hours, X4 is hands down the greatest, full-package, space sim experience I have ever played in my life, and I'm not sure anything will ever attain what they've done here. A fully real-time, galactic simulation of thousands of NPC's engaging in an ever-evolving tug-of-war for resources and property, with an ever-looming, very real threat of galactic genocide by a rouge AI race of machines.

It's all the best stuff of sci-fi in a single experience, and I wouldn't trade it for anything else.

The number of systems that work together to lead to such deep and complex emergent is truly unfounded. The game is unforgiving in how obtuse and confusing its learning curve is. But for those that are willing to stick through it and truly rewarded with an gaming experience like no other. Moreover, I also find exceptionally enjoyable to see myself becoming more familiar with the complex UI and controls. It honestly feels like learning to use the UI is part of the game itself LOL and is a skill in and of itself that you can master. 

You start off in a small ship, doing missions and building up wealth to hire miners and traders. Its fascinating how you go from being the cockpit yourself, shooting down pirates, Xenon and K'haak threats. To then transitioning into station building and fleet management. The game never lets up in its complexity. Even at the point of just building station, eventually you realize you can create complex supply logistic networks. For exampls, ships can be coordinated between stations you own to create large supply chains to funnel raw resources from distant, asteroid filled sectors to more central production sectors where you can construct large megaplexes to provide other factions with manufactured goods or eventually build your own ships. 

Combat never lets up either. You go from one ships to sending commands to small patrols. Eventually you get bigger ships with more firepower. However, even the small S class ships you used at the start never lose their importance. Carrier can carry large squad of S class ships that you inidividually build and outfit YOURSELF using your massive, complex production and logistics network. These ships can provide support to your larger capital ships, taking out surface elements on enemy capitals such as turrets, shields and even engines which cripples their ability to retaliate while your capitals rip them apart. Or, S class ships can be outfitted as bomber squads, delivering massive payloads to any target that you have. Combat is really however you want to engage it.

Its is truly incredible how many systems work together to create such deep and complex emergent gameplay. Thank you Egosoft for creating a truly one of a kind gaming experience.
